sound_type: tooting whistled series -- evenly spaced, hollow 'toot' notes at ~800 Hz, ~0.5 notes per second; slower and more deliberate than Saw-whet; diurnal calling; E=3 quiet but persistent
ecosystem: Open coniferous and mixed forest, forest edges, mountain meadow margins. Year-round resident. Cavity nester. Largely diurnal. Fearless predator hunting songbirds and small mammals.
season: Year-round resident. Calling peaks February-June (breeding). Diurnal hunting year-round. Non-migratory.
family: Strigidae (9th consecutive: 135-143. TIES PICIDAE NONET RECORD)

coordinates.description: Northern Pygmy-Owls resident in forested areas of the Cascades and Olympics. Found at forest edges and mountain meadow margins.

taxonomy_note.description: First Northern Pygmy-Owl entry. Ninth consecutive Strigidae -- TIES Picidae nonet record. Sixth genus (Glaucidium). PNW's smallest owl. False eyes (occipital eyespots). Fearless predator hunting above its weight. Diurnal. E=3 quiet but persistent tooting.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Strigidae: tenth entry total. Nine consecutive at 135-143. TIES Picidae nonet.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Glaucidium from Greek 'glaukidion' (little owl, from 'glaukos' meaning gleaming). gnoma from Greek 'gnoma' (mark, sign) -- perhaps referring to the occipital eyespots.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Glaucidium -- first entry. Sixth genus in the Strigidae run (after Bubo, Strix, Asio, Aegolius, Megascops).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Strigiformes. Tenth entry. Nine consecutive at 135-143.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Strigidae TIES Picidae nonet record at nine consecutive. The all-time mark is matched. Degree 144 will BREAK the record.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Primary tooting: ~800 Hz. Lower than Saw-whet (~1000 Hz). Higher than Screech-Owl (400-600 Hz).
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Calling bouts: 30 seconds to several minutes. Individual notes: ~0.3 seconds with ~1.7 seconds silence.
song_metrics.note_structure: TOOTING SERIES: Evenly spaced, hollow 'toot' notes. ~0.5 notes per second. Slower and more deliberate than Saw-whet. Each note a single clean whistle.
song_metrics.note_types: Tooting series (primary), rapid trill (territorial/courtship), contact call.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Series of evenly spaced single notes. No acceleration (unlike Screech-Owl). No continuous running (unlike Saw-whet). Deliberate, measured.
song_metrics.timbre: Hollow, clear, whistled. Slightly lower and more resonant than Saw-whet. The deliberate spacing gives each note presence.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Moderate. Carries through open forest. Similar range to Saw-whet and Screech-Owl. Forest-scale, not landscape-scale.
song_metrics.tempo: ~0.5 notes per second. The slowest tooting in the Strigidae run. Deliberate. Each note separated by ~1.7 seconds of silence.
song_metrics.context: Territorial advertisement, courtship, pair contact. Diurnal calling. Often triggers immediate mobbing response from songbirds.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Some variation in note rate and pitch across range.
song_metrics.key_property: DELIBERATE TOOTING -- E=3 SLOW, PERSISTENT. ~800 Hz, ~0.5 notes/sec. TIES PICIDAE NONET (9th consecutive Strigidae). Sixth genus (Glaucidium). PNW's smallest owl. False eyes. Fearless predator. Diurnal. Triggers intense mobbing.

plumage.overall: Brown above with white spotting. White below with brown streaking. Round head, no ear tufts. Yellow eyes. Long tail. OCCIPITAL EYESPOTS: dark spots bordered by white on the back of the head, mimicking eyes.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Occipital eyespots (false eyes on back of head). Small size. Long tail. Round head. No ear tufts. Yellow eyes.
plumage.flight_pattern: Fast, direct, undulating. Agile through forest canopy. Active pursuit of prey. Long tail provides maneuverability.

physical_description.overall: Length: 15-18 cm. Weight: 54-73 g. Wingspan: 30-36 cm. PNW's smallest owl. Smaller than a robin. Long tail relative to body.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Tiny owl with long tail, round head, no ear tufts, and occipital eyespots. If songbirds are mobbing aggressively at a point in the forest, a Pygmy-Owl is likely present.
physical_description.field_id: Listen for slow, deliberate tooting in open forest during daytime. Follow mobbing songbirds to locate the owl. The false eyes on the back of the head are visible at close range. The long tail and tiny size are diagnostic.

nodes.title: TIES Picidae Nonet Record (9th Consecutive Strigidae)
nodes.description: Nine consecutive Strigidae entries (135-143). Six genera. Seven species. Ties the Picidae nonet as the all-time longest family streak. Degree 144 will break the record.

nodes.title: Occipital Eyespots -- False Eyes
nodes.description: Dark spots bordered by white on the back of the head mimic a watching face. Anti-predator deception. May confuse mobbing songbirds. Convergent evolution in small owls worldwide.

nodes.title: Fearless Micro-Predator
nodes.description: PNW's smallest owl (54-73g) hunts prey its own size or larger. Active pursuit through forest canopy. Diurnal. Triggers intense mobbing from dozens of songbirds. The most feared small predator.

nodes.title: Diurnal Hunter (Second in Run)
nodes.description: Second diurnal owl in the Strigidae run (after Short-eared at 140). Forest hunter vs. grassland hunter. Hunts at dawn, dusk, and full daylight. Visible to human observers.

nodes.title: E=3 Triptych (141-142-143)
nodes.description: Three consecutive E=3 entries. Saw-whet (metronomic), Screech-Owl (musical), Pygmy-Owl (contemplative). Three flavors of quiet. Energy descent from E=6 peak at 138.

cross_references.relationship: SMALL OWL PAIR! Both tiny (Saw-whet 54-151g, Pygmy-Owl 54-73g). Both toot. But Saw-whet hunts rodents, Pygmy-Owl hunts birds. Nocturnal vs. diurnal. Different ecological strategies at similar size.
DIURNAL PAIR! Both hunt during the day. Short-eared: open grassland, bark + wing-clap (E=5). Pygmy-Owl: forest, tooting (E=3). Two diurnal strategies.
MASS RATIO! GHO (~1400g) to Pygmy-Owl (~62g) = 22:1. Largest to smallest owl within nine consecutive Strigidae degrees.
NONET TIE! Nine consecutive Strigidae (135-143). Six genera. Seven species. Ties Picidae nonet record. Degree 144 breaks it.
